text:
PM Anthony Albanese was making the rounds on breakfast television on Thursday when he was questioned about a national public holiday to celebrate Australia's most golden Olympics ever.

Prime Minister Anthony Albanese has laughed off a suggestion for a new national public holiday to recognise Australia making history at the Paris Games overnight.

It was one of the most golden days for Australia in the Olympics, after claiming gold in skateboarding, sailing, track cycling and the pole vault on Wednesday (local time).

The wins takes the gold medal count to 18, marking the most successful Games for Australia in its history after taking home 17 gold in Tokyo and 16 in Sydney.

Two bronze medals were also picked up by Matt Denny in the discus, the first for an Australian in the event, and relay walk team of Jemima Montag and Rhydian Cowley.

Making the rounds on breakfast television on Wednesday to speak about the 15 per cent in pay increase for childcare workers, the PM was pressed about a public holiday.

"Could we have a day off to celebrate?" Sunrise host Matt Shirvington asked.

Mr Albanese laughed it off and told Shirvington to "negotiate" with management.

"Certainly there are many Australians who will be pretty tired after the last couple of weeks because they have been engaged with our athletes," the Labor leader said.

"And the thing about these Olympians as well, it's important to remember, is that for so many of them, they get by on not much income at all.

"They're not receiving the sort of salaries we see our... rugby league or AFL players receive. They do it out of their joy for their sport (and) their love for their country."

Mr Albanese also noted how Australia was constantly "punching way above our weight" in terms of medals won per capita at the Olympics.

"(We have) 27 million people compared with the hundreds of millions in the United States and... the more than a million in China and India," he said.

"It's quite an extraordinary achievement."

Australia has won a total of 41 medals in the 12 days of competition.

The history-making day started with competitive sailor Matt Wearn winning his second consecutive gold in the men's dinghy.

The event at the Bay of Marseilles was hit by delays and postponed on Wednesday due to a wind shift, but hours later it resumed with Wearn declared the winner.

"It hasn't really sunk in. I thought I'd break down pretty quickly but it's just pure excitement. It's something no one's done before, going back to back in the ILCA (dinghy) – or the laser as it was before," the 28-year-old said after the race.

"That was a massive goal and I've made it happen so it's pretty special."

The gold was replicated a couple of hours later by Keegan Palmer, who defended his title from the Tokyo Games, triumphing in the men's skateboarding.

The 21-year-old set the challenge on his first run with a blistering score of 93.11, which was enough for him to secure back-to-back golds.

"I got really lucky though by everyone kind of falling off on their last two runs, but I had something prepared if need be," American-born Palmer said.

"I'm speechless though. I just can't believe it bro. Everyone's yelling at me. I'm just happy. Come on Australia, let's do this."

He was watched on by US skateboarding legend Tony Hawke and rapper Snoop Dogg.

Later, the Australian men's track cycling team beat Great Britain in a tense race.

It was back and forth between the teams with Australia marginally ahead, but when its rider Brit Ethan Hayter slid off the saddle Australia took advantage.

Team Australia starring Oliver Bleddyn, Sam Welsford, Conor Leahy and Kelland O'Brien crossed the line to win gold with a time of 3:42.067.

Then just before 6am, Nina Kennedy capped off Australia's golden day winning the women's pole vaulting comfortably clearing 4.90m.

Her American rival Katie Moon - who she shared gold with at the World Championships in Budapest last year - could only hit 4.85m.

The 27-year-old is the first Australian to win an Olympic field title, after struggling with injuries, depression and anxiety as she rose through the ranks.
